exercises.title: The GenAI span categories
exercises.prompt: OpenTelemetry's GenAI SIG defines the standard schema for agent telemetry. Name the three GenAI span categories (model/client, agent, tool) and the top-level attributes (provider name, request model, data-source ID). Why does a standard schema matter across vendors?
exercises.hint: Span categories: model/client (an LLM call), agent (an agent invocation), tool (a tool execution). Top-level attrs: gen_ai.provider.name, gen_ai.request.model, data-source ID. Standard schema = traces mean the same thing in Datadog, Grafana, Jaeger, Honeycomb — portable, comparable telemetry.
exercises.solution:
**The three GenAI span categories** (OpenTelemetry GenAI semantic conventions, GenAI SIG launched April 2024):
- **Model/client spans:** an **LLM call** — a request to a model (the completion/chat call). Captures the model interaction (provider, model, tokens, parameters). The lowest-level GenAI operation.
- **Agent spans:** an **agent invocation** — an agent's execution (the agent loop, Lesson 1). Captures the agent-level operation (which agent, the overall invocation). Wraps the model and tool spans of one agent run. (Distinguished into `invoke_agent` CLIENT vs INTERNAL spans depending on whether it's an external call to an agent or an internal step — next concept.)
- **Tool spans:** a **tool execution** — a tool call the agent makes (Phase 13). Captures which tool ran, its result, timing.
These three categories — **model (LLM calls), agent (agent runs), tool (tool executions)** — form the **span hierarchy** of an agent trace (Phase 13.20): agent span → model spans + tool spans nested within it, reconstructing the agent's perceive-reason-act loop (Lesson 1) as a navigable trace.

**Top-level GenAI attributes:**
- **Provider name** (`gen_ai.provider.name`): which provider (anthropic/openai/etc.) served the call.
- **Request model** (`gen_ai.request.model`): which model was requested.
- **Data-source ID:** identifies the data source (for RAG/retrieval operations — which knowledge base/index).
(Plus token usage, operation name, and others — Phase 13.20.) These standard attributes ensure every GenAI span carries **consistent, agreed-upon metadata**.

**Why a standard schema matters across vendors:** the OTel GenAI conventions define **standard span names, attributes, and content-capture rules** so that **agent traces mean the same thing across all observability vendors** — Datadog, Grafana, Jaeger, Honeycomb, Langfuse, Phoenix (Lesson 24), etc. This standardization is essential because:
- **Portability / no lock-in:** if you instrument with the **OTel GenAI standard**, your traces work in **any compliant backend** — you can **switch observability vendors without re-instrumenting** (the same standardization benefit as MCP for tools, Phase 13.6). Vendor-neutral.
- **Consistency / comparability:** a `gen_ai.usage.input_tokens` attribute **means the same thing everywhere** — so traces are **comparable across tools, teams, and vendors**. A trace exported to Datadog and one to Langfuse have the **same structure and semantics** — you can query, compare, and reason about them uniformly.
- **Ecosystem interoperability:** frameworks (LangGraph, etc.) and platforms (Lesson 24) can all **emit and consume the same standard** — so the whole agent-observability ecosystem interoperates (the same 'one standard unlocks the ecosystem' as MCP, A2A, Phase 13). 
Without a standard, every framework/vendor would emit incompatible telemetry, and you couldn't build coherent traces or switch backends. The OTel GenAI conventions are the **standardization that makes agent observability portable, comparable, and ecosystem-wide** — exactly what Phase 13.20 established for the tools stack, here for agents.

exercises.title: invoke_agent CLIENT vs INTERNAL, and a span hierarchy
exercises.prompt: Distinguish invoke_agent CLIENT spans from INTERNAL spans and when each applies. Build a trace hierarchy covering the agent loop, an LLM call, a tool call, and an MCP client dispatch using OTel GenAI conventions. Explain the content-capture rules (opt-in vs redact).
exercises.hint: CLIENT span: the agent is invoked as an external service (a call OUT to an agent). INTERNAL span: an agent step within the same process. Hierarchy: agent span (root) → LLM model span + tool span (→ MCP dispatch). Content capture: span structure/metadata always; prompt/completion content opt-in (default redacted) for privacy.
exercises.solution:
**invoke_agent CLIENT vs INTERNAL spans:**
- **CLIENT span:** used when the **agent is invoked as an external service** — i.e. you're making a **call *out* to an agent** (across a process/service boundary, like calling a remote agent or a sub-agent service). The CLIENT span represents 'I called an external agent' (the client side of a remote agent invocation) — analogous to an outbound RPC/HTTP CLIENT span.
- **INTERNAL span:** used when the **agent invocation is a step *within* the same process** — an **internal agent operation** (a sub-step of the agent's own execution, not crossing a service boundary). It represents internal work within the current process.
**When each applies:** use **CLIENT** when crossing a **service boundary** (calling a separate agent service, a remote sub-agent, an A2A delegation, Phase 13.19) — it's an outbound call to another component. Use **INTERNAL** when the agent step is **in-process** (the agent's own internal steps). The distinction mirrors OTel's general CLIENT (outbound cross-boundary call) vs INTERNAL (in-process operation) span kinds — it tells you whether an agent invocation **left your process** (CLIENT, a distributed hop) or stayed **inside** (INTERNAL), which matters for understanding the distributed structure of a multi-agent system.

**A trace hierarchy (OTel GenAI conventions):**
```python
with tracer.span("invoke_agent", kind="INTERNAL",                  # ROOT: agent loop
                 attrs={"gen_ai.agent.name": "researcher"}):
    with tracer.span("chat", kind="CLIENT",                        # LLM model span
                     attrs={"gen_ai.provider.name": "anthropic",
                            "gen_ai.request.model": "claude",
                            "gen_ai.usage.input_tokens": 1200}):
        ...  # the LLM call
    with tracer.span("execute_tool", kind="INTERNAL",             # tool span
                     attrs={"gen_ai.tool.name": "search_docs"}):
        with tracer.span("mcp.dispatch", kind="CLIENT",           # MCP client dispatch
                         attrs={"mcp.server": "docs-server"}):
            ...  # MCP client routes to the server (cross-boundary → CLIENT)
```
The hierarchy: **agent span (root, INTERNAL — the loop) → LLM model span (CLIENT — calling the model provider) + tool span (INTERNAL — the tool execution) → MCP dispatch span (CLIENT — routing out to the MCP server)**. The nesting captures the agent's structure (Lesson 1's loop: agent → LLM → tool), and the CLIENT/INTERNAL kinds capture which operations cross boundaries (LLM provider call, MCP server call = CLIENT) vs stay in-process (the agent loop, tool execution = INTERNAL). This reconstructs the full agent execution as one trace (Phase 13.20).

**Content-capture rules (opt-in vs redact):** the OTel GenAI conventions specify (Phase 13.20):
- **Always captured (safe):** **span structure and metadata** — span names, hierarchy, **timings/durations**, **status/errors**, **token usage**, **model/provider**, tool names. Operational metadata with no sensitive content.
- **Opt-in (default REDACTED):** the **actual content** — **prompts, completions, tool arguments, tool results**. Content capture is **off by default** because it **frequently contains sensitive data** (PII, secrets, user data, proprietary info). Capturing it by default and shipping to an observability backend would be a **privacy/security disaster** (Phase 13.20). So content is **redacted by default**, captured only when **explicitly enabled** (and ideally with PII-scrubbing).
**Why this rule:** you get **operational visibility (timings, tokens, structure, errors) without leaking content** — privacy-by-default observability. The conventions standardize **what's safe to capture (metadata) vs what needs opt-in (content)** so that every vendor handles the privacy boundary the same way (Phase 13.20's privacy-respecting observability). This lets you trace and debug agents while keeping sensitive content out of the telemetry pipeline unless deliberately enabled.

exercises.title: Standardized agent observability, why it's load-bearing, and the platform ecosystem
exercises.prompt: Explain why standardized agent observability (OTel GenAI) is load-bearing for production agents (debugging non-deterministic distributed systems, cost, latency, regressions), how it enables the platform ecosystem (Lesson 24's Langfuse/Phoenix/Opik), why it composes with the MCP/tool observability (Phase 13.20), and the limits of tracing alone.
exercises.hint: Load-bearing: agents are non-deterministic distributed systems that fail silently — tracing is the only way to see what happened. Standard schema → emit once, any platform consumes (Langfuse/Phoenix/Opik). Composes with Phase 13.20 (same OTel GenAI standard across tools+agents). Limits: tracing shows WHAT happened, not whether it's correct (need evals).
exercises.solution:
**Why standardized agent observability is load-bearing for production agents:** agents are **non-deterministic, distributed systems that fail silently** (Phase 11.10, 13.20) — and you **cannot operate them without observability**:
- **Debugging non-deterministic distributed systems:** an agent fans out across **LLM calls, tools, MCP servers, sub-agents** (Lesson 1, Phase 13) — a distributed system. And it's **non-deterministic** (the same input can produce different runs). When something goes wrong, the **only way to see what actually happened is the trace** — which LLM call decided what, which tools ran, where it failed. Without the trace, agent failures are **unreproducible and opaque** (Phase 11.10). Tracing is the *only* lens.
- **Silent failure:** agents **fail silently and plausibly** (fluent wrong answers, no crash, Phase 11.10) — there's **no error signal** unless you instrument. Tracing (+ evals) is how you detect that anything went wrong.
- **Cost:** token-usage attributes per LLM span enable **cost tracking** (Phase 11.11) — find what's expensive.
- **Latency:** span durations reveal **where time goes** (slow tool, slow model, slow MCP server) — essential for optimization (especially voice, Lesson 22).
- **Regressions:** trace metrics over time **detect quality/cost/latency regressions** when you change prompts/models/tools (Phase 11.10).
Observability is **load-bearing** — it's what makes agents **debuggable, cost-controllable, and reliable**. The 89% of organizations with agent observability by 2026 (Lesson 24) reflect that it's now **table stakes** for production agents.

**How it enables the platform ecosystem (Lesson 24's Langfuse/Phoenix/Opik):** because OTel GenAI is a **standard schema**, you can **emit conformant traces once** and **any compliant platform can consume them** — which is what enables the **observability platform ecosystem**:
- **Langfuse, Arize Phoenix, Comet Opik** (Lesson 24) all **natively support OTel GenAI / OpenInference** — so you instrument with the standard, and **any of these platforms ingests and visualizes your traces** (plus their own value-adds: Langfuse's prompt management/sessions, Phoenix's RAG evals/auto-instrumentation, Opik's optimization/guardrails). 
- **No lock-in:** you can **switch platforms** without re-instrumenting (emit OTel, swap the backend). The standard **decouples instrumentation from the platform**.
The standard is what makes the **platform ecosystem interoperable** — emit once, choose your platform, switch freely (the standardization → ecosystem pattern, Phase 13.6/13.20).

**Why it composes with MCP/tool observability (Phase 13.20):** OTel GenAI is the **same standard** Phase 13.20 established for the **tools/MCP stack** — so **agent observability and tool observability use the same conventions**, and **one trace spans both**:
- An agent trace (this lesson — agent → LLM → tool spans) **stitches together with** the MCP-stack spans (Phase 13.20 — MCP client dispatch, server execution) and A2A sub-agent spans (Phase 13.19) — all emitting the **same OTel GenAI conventions**. 
- So you get **end-to-end traces across the whole stack**: agent loop → LLM calls → MCP tool calls → MCP servers → A2A sub-agents → gateways (Phase 13.17) — all in **one coherent, standard trace** (W3C trace propagation, Lesson 17 carries the context). 
The agent layer (this lesson) and the tool/protocol layer (Phase 13.20) **compose into one observability story** because they share the standard — exactly the point of standardization. You instrument the whole agent-and-tools system uniformly.

**The limits of tracing alone:** tracing is necessary but **not sufficient**:
- **Tracing shows *what happened*, not whether it's *correct*:** a trace tells you the agent called these tools, took this long, cost this much, and returned this output — but it **can't tell you if the output is *right*** (correct, helpful, safe). A trace of a fluent-wrong-answer looks fine (Phase 11.10's silent failure). **You need evaluation** (Lesson 30, Phase 10.10/11.10) — running the output against correctness criteria — to know if the agent is *working*, not just *what it did*. Tracing is the **'what happened' layer**; evals are the **'was it good' layer**.
- **Tracing doesn't fix anything** — it gives visibility; you still need to **act on it** (debug, optimize, add evals/guardrails).
- **Content redaction (Exercise 2)** means traces by default don't show *what* was said (only metadata) — for content-level debugging you must opt in (with privacy care).
So the full observability stack is **tracing (OTel GenAI — what happened, standardized, portable) + evaluation (was it correct — Lesson 30) + monitoring/alerting (catch regressions)**. The synthesis: **standardized agent observability (OTel GenAI) is the load-bearing 'what happened' foundation** — it makes non-deterministic distributed agents **debuggable, cost-trackable, latency-analyzable, and regression-detectable**, enables a **portable platform ecosystem (Lesson 24)**, and **composes with the tool/MCP observability (Phase 13.20) into one end-to-end trace** — but it must be **paired with evaluation** (Lesson 30) to answer not just 'what did the agent do?' but 'did it do it *right*?' Tracing tells you the agent's behavior; evals tell you if that behavior is correct — together they're how you operate reliable production agents.
